text: The camera view shows the diaphragm at the top of the frame, with the old stomach visible below. The surgeon lifts a small amount of fat to reveal the liver underneath, while the spleen is located in the corner of the view.

The surgeon begins creating the gastric pouch by cutting and stapling the stomach tissue. A surgical device, similar in size to a pen and pencil, is used to cut and place tiny staples, sealing and dividing the tissue. This process is repeated three times to form the new pouch on the left, while the old stomach remains on the right. A retractor elevates the liver, which appears healthy and brown, providing a clear view of the surgical area.

The surgeon completes the first step of the gastric bypass procedure by making three staple firings to create a new, smaller stomach pouch. The old stomach is visible for comparison, showing the size difference. The surgeon then cuts through the apron-like fat layer to create a passage for later intestinal repositioning.

The surgeon cuts through the apron of fat and lifts it to expose the colon. Beneath the colon, the pancreas is visible, and below the pancreas, the red intestine transitions into the blue intestine. The surgeon pulls the intestine and uses staplers to cut it at the identified spot. The intestines are measured further down, approximately three feet, where the two parts are attached using staplers.

You can see we put some staples in in a minute. There's always a chance that something can leak out, that the staples don't seal well, that the sutures don't heal well, and that things can leak out. It's called a leak. When the juices from the inside leak out, they are kind of toxic. So then people get something called peritonitis. See, when this is the inside here, if it leaks out, something gets peritonitis. peritonitis and can lead to sepsis, all these bad words. And then people usually need another surgery to fix the problem. So this leak is a very rare thing to happen. The last time it happened for me was like years and years and years ago. So we staple this close, close it, but again, as I said, if these don't seal well, you could develop a leak.

The other thing that can happen on any time we operate on the intestines or any time we operate on a patient is something called scar tissue. The medical word is adhesions. And adhesions can also, they work like spider web. They can get like a blockage. And then you might need surgery and we'll fix it. Another reason is here you can see two pieces of fat. In the middle is like a little hole. It's like a slit. And the intestines can get pinched in that slit. When they get pinched, again, you get a blockage, you need surgery. We haven't seen this for quite a while because what we do is we'll suture this up here right now. Suture it closed so it won't happen. But some people lose so much fat that this actually melts away and they develop that hole again and then they get the problem. But it's a rare thing to happen because we suture it. We don't like complications, so we try to prevent as much as we can. So double and triple check so that you don't have any problems because I don't want any problems.

The surgeon prepares to attach the unattached end of the intestine to the newly created gastric pouch. The intestine is pulled up towards the pouch, and the attachment is performed using sutures, with four stitches placed—two at the back and two at the front. This technique is chosen to minimize complications such as wound infections, strictures, and ulcers, which are more common with stapler methods.

The surgeon creates a small hole in the upper and lower parts of the intestine, preparing to attach them for food passage. The hole is as small as a pencil diameter. An endoscope is used to perform a leak test, ensuring the sutured connection is secure and not too tight. The surgeon continues suturing the front and back sides, followed by another leak test to confirm proper healing.

The surgeon retracts the endoscope while simultaneously inflating air to check for leaks, observing for any air bubbles that would indicate a leak. The video shows five small incisions on the abdomen, including one at the belly button.
